danger_signals.visual_form: Spike/Blackie character approaching player grid position
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent collision; loss of life if contact occurs
Instant death hazard; must avoid or door-trap enemy

danger_signals.visual_form: Destructible walls/pillars collapsing after hammer strikes
danger_signals.meaning: Player can be knocked to bottom of playfield if struck by falling debris
Potential trap state; level must be aborted if Mario becomes trapped

danger_signals.visual_form: Isolated destructible walls after premature destruction of access ladders
danger_signals.meaning: Level failure state; game progression blocked

safety_signals.meaning: Enemy redirect mechanism; opens pathway for harmless enemy routing into background

safety_signals.visual_form: Empty spaces where destructible objects previously existed
safety_signals.meaning: Progress toward level completion; safe movement corridors expanding
Score multiplier; indicates successful destruction chain strategy
Safety margin; player starts with five lives buffer

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Occupies center of grid-based playfield; active destruction agent

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Construction foreman character sprite; identified as opponent in original arcade
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Single named antagonist; appears as mobile threat across levels
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Spawns from enemy doors; moves across playfield attempting to disrupt player; knocks down objects and forces falls

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Full playfield grid populated with destructible walls, pillars, ladders, indestructible barrels, bombs, and enemy spawn doors
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Mario positioned at safe starting location; enemies contained behind doors awaiting activation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Real-time destruction of target objects via hammer strikes; falling debris animation; enemy movement across grid
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Spike pursues Mario; player must optimize destruction order; chain-destruction combinations trigger bonus scoring

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Spike sprite actively moving toward Mario's position; fireballs spawning from antagonist
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player forced into evasion priority over object destruction; strategic door usage required to redirect enemy into background

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: All required destructible objects removed from playfield; remaining grid shows empty spaces and indestructible elements only
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player advances to next level; life counter preserved or incremented based on bonus points

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Mario surrounded by indestructible barrels or unreachable destructible objectives; no ladder access visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Level must be aborted; game respawns at level start or life counter decrements

environmental_storytelling.example: Invisible grid dividing playfield communicates puzzle logic: each space holds one object type, constraining player movement and destruction sequencing; reinforces 'construction site demolition' theme through structured environment

Destructible walls vs. indestructible barrels vs. bombs vs. ladders establish role-based ecosystem: walls = targets, barrels = obstacles, bombs = chain-reaction tools, ladders = access. Visual distinction teaches systemic rules through environment design

environmental_storytelling.technique: Enemy door mechanic as spatial control
environmental_storytelling.example: Doors opening to release enemies, then closing to route them 'harmlessly into background,' communicates containment/control narrative: player is construction worker managing hazardous environment through smart positioning

Mario unable to jump due to hammer weight; falling objects endanger player; destruction order determines safe movement paths. Communicates 'weight and consequence' theme: every action has spatial ripple effects

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Positioned on stable ladder or platform; awaits player input; no animation
Hammer raised; destruction animation plays on target object; momentary freeze before next action
Gravity animation; character descends to bottom of playfield when support removed; loss of life if enemy contact occurs during fall
Moves systematically across grid toward player position; destroys objects in path to disrupt navigation
Accelerates pursuit when player is in line of sight; prioritizes close-quarters engagement; knocks Mario downward
Exits through open door; moves into background layer (visual depth cue indicating removal from active threat space)
Flash animation; crumble and disappear; adjacent objects may fall if support removed
Static obstacle; never destroyed; visual marker for permanent playfield constraints
Chain-reaction; destroys all connected destructible objects in visual area; bonus point multiplier triggers

notes: Wrecking Crew '98 is a competitive sequel to the 1985 original, shifting from single-player demolition puzzles to multiplayer block-based competition. The Wikipedia source focuses primarily on the original 1984/1985 game; specific visual communication details for the '98 SNES sequel are limited. The manifestation patterns extracted apply primarily to the original's grid-based puzzle architecture, which likely carries forward to '98 with competitive gameplay overlays. Key world-state communication relies on spatial (grid position), object-type (destructible vs. indestructible), and behavioral (enemy proximity, falling physics) visual cues rather than narrative exposition. The game communicates danger through immediate threat proximity and mechanical consequence rather than abstract indicators.
